step.title: Step 95
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 195, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player M at slot 2 (rows 24-29), cols 11-16 - N (green) at slot 2, cols 36-39 (carried - closest to player) - R (red) at slot 2, cols 42-45 - b (blue) at slot 3, cols 42-45 - Bottom bar shows desired order: R, N, b from left to right - Valid target columns: 18, 24, 30, 36, 42 - Goal: R→col 24, N→col 30, b→col 36
- Recent findings: ACTION7 doesn't work. Both N and R follow the player between slots. N is the carried target (leftmost). New hypothesis: If I move the player to slot 3 where b is, maybe b becomes the carried target since it's the only target in that slot (besides N and R which follow). Let me test by moving DOWN to slot 3 and trying LEFT/RIGHT.
- Open questions: - How to switch carried target from N to R? Need to test if N can move past R or if R becomes carried when N moves right.
- Plan: Test moving N RIGHT to see what happens when it reaches R's position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 194 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Nothing changed! M is still at the same position (rows 30-35, cols 11-16), and all blocks are in the same positions. The RIGHT and DOWN actions didn't move M at all. This is very strange.
Wait - M is at cols 11-16. Looking at the images, M is positioned right next to the vertical gray strip on the left. Maybe M is stuck because there's a wall to the right? Or maybe M can only move UP/DOWN along the left side?
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Looking at the images:
- There's a vertical gray strip on the left (cols ~9-11)
- M is at cols 11-16, adjacent to this strip
- The gray play area extends to the right
- Blocks R, N, b are in the play area
Maybe M can only move UP and DOWN along the left edge, and the blocks respond to M's position? When M moves to a certain row/slot, blocks on that slot move?
Let me look at what happened before. Earlier, M was able to move RIGHT and carry blocks. But now M seems stuck.
Actually, wait - maybe M's position is fixed at cols 11-16 and M can only move UP/DOWN. The RIGHT/LEFT movements I was doing earlier might have been moving blocks, not M itself. Let me re-examine the history.
Looking at the sequence of images:
- Step 184: M at rows 24-29, R at some position
- Step 185: M at rows 24-29, R moved right
- Step 186: M at rows 24-29, R moved further right
- Step 187: M at rows 24-29, R at cols 42-45
- Step 188: M at rows 30-35 (after DOWN), N moved to slot 3
So M CAN move UP/DOWN. When M moved DOWN from slot 1 to slot 2, N got pushed to slot 3. But M is still at cols 11-16.
So M is constrained to the left side (cols 11-16) and can only move UP/DOWN. The blocks are in the play area to the right. When M moves to a slot, blocks on that slot respond somehow.
Let me think about this differently. The goal is to arrange R, N, b in the order shown on the bottom bar. Currently:
- Slot 2: R (cols 42-45)
- Slot 3: N (cols 18-21), b (cols 42-45)
The bottom bar shows: M, R, N, b (from left to right).
Maybe the goal is to have the blocks arranged in the order R, N, b from left to right on the board. Currently reading left-to-right: N (col 18), R (col 42), b (col 42) - but R and b overlap in columns.
Wait, R is on slot 2 and b is on slot 3. If I read by slot first, then by column:
- Slot 2: R
- Slot 3: N, b
Order: R, N, b - that matches the goal!
But maybe the level isn't complete yet. Let me check if there's some completion condition I'm missing.
Actually, maybe I need to get all three blocks on the SAME slot in the order R, N, b. Currently they're spread across slots 2 and 3.
Let me try to get R down to slot 3 where N and b are. If I move M to slot 3, maybe R will follow or get pushed down.
But M can't move DOWN from slot 2 to slot 3 (tried and it didn't work). Maybe I need to move M UP first to slot 1, then somehow get R to slot 3?
Actually, let me re-examine. M is at rows 30-35 (slot 2). When I tried DOWN, nothing happened. Maybe slot 3 is at a different row range that M can't reach?
Let me look at where N and b are: rows 37-40. So slot 3 is rows 37-40. M is at rows 30-35. The gap between 35 and 37 is only 1-2 rows. Maybe there's a barrier.
Let me try moving M UP to slot 1 and see what happens to the blocks.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: M didn't move at all - RIGHT/LEFT don't work from current position. M seems constrained to move only UP/DOWN along the left edge. Let me try moving UP to slot 1 to see how blocks respond.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving M UP to slot 1
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
board_blocks = []
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['M', 'R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] >= 16:
        rows = [r for r, c in n['boundary']]
        cols = [c for r, c in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            board_blocks.append((n['color'], min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))
print("Board blocks after UP:")
for b in board_blocks:
    print(f"  {b}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
